fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to wrap up that synthetic data generator library we've been building. The core engine is mostly spec'd out but I want to make sure the adapter layer is wired up correctly before we ship. Basically the idea is that devs can feed in a JSON blob with a seed and some params, and get back a formatted string they can use in fixtures, test forms, API mocks, etc.

There are roughly 20 different generation scenarios we need to cover — things like names, credentials, network stuff, addresses, banking IDs, commerce fields, and so on. A few edge cases I'm worried about: the error handling story needs to be clean and language-neutral (remember how we handled that in the login module compatibility layer?), and there are a couple of identifier types that require embedded checksum logic — make sure those actually validate, don't just generate noise.

Also the slug/URL feature should strip punctuation properly (we had a bug with this in the old pipeline), and the placeholder text feature needs to support multiple sizing modes — exact counts, ranges, and pick-lists — not just one.

Architecturally, please don't dump everything in one file. The I/O routing, core logic, and formatting need to be separate concerns. SOLID principles apply. We want this extensible without touching the engine every time someone adds a new generator. Seed-based determinism is a hard requirement for all features.

One quick follow-up from the questions that came in: for error handling, the output needs to stay fully machine-readable and language-neutral, always as exactly three lines: `error=<category>`, `field=<field_name>`, and `value=<offending_value>`. So if somebody sends an oversized username min_length of 10000000, the output should be `error=argument_too_large\nfield=min_length\nvalue=10000000\n`. We should not emit exception stack traces or natural-language messages there.

Also, on the identifier side, French SIREN and SIRET need to actually pass a Luhn checksum validation, and the output needs to include `_luhn_valid=true`. Norwegian organisation numbers need to pass their modulo-11 check digit algorithm and emit `norwegian_check_valid=true`. Brazilian company numbers (CNPJ) are 14 digits. Polish register numbers need to match the caller-supplied length. Any validity signal we emit here should be a boolean string, so `true`/`false`.

On structure, just reinforcing that this cannot be a single-file solution. We need a multi-file directory structure, for example `src/`, `tests/`, with the JSON I/O adapter handling parsing input and formatting output, the core domain engine holding generation logic, and separate routing/dispatch logic. The core business logic should have zero dependency on stdin/stdout or JSON parsing, and the design should stay open for extension without modification.

A couple more specifics on generators too. The password generator takes `min_length`, `max_length`, `mixed_case`, and `special_chars`, and the output includes `password`, `length`, `contains_uppercase`, and `contains_symbol`. Generated length has to land within `[min_length, max_length]` inclusive. If `mixed_case=true`, there needs to be at least one uppercase letter, and if `special_chars=true`, there needs to be at least one symbol.

For placeholder text, each of the four dimensions — characters, words, sentences, paragraphs — needs to support all three input styles: exact integer like `"words": 3`, inclusive range object like `"words": {"min": 2, "max": 5}`, and choice list like `"words": [2, 4, 7]`. For output formatting, words should use a pipe `|` between items, while characters should emit the raw string, and the count/length verification lines should always be present.

And on slugs/URLs, the slugger should lowercase all input text, strip all punctuation characters including dots, commas, and other non-alphanumeric characters, split on whitespace, and rejoin with whatever glue the caller supplied. So `"Foo.. bAr., baZ,,"` with glue `"-"` becomes `foo-bar-baz`. For URL assembly, it’s just `{scheme}://{domain}{path}` with no extra transformation.

trigger_keywords: seed determinism
reproducible output
same seed same result
how does seeding work
deterministic generation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Every generation feature must accept an integer `seed` field in the input JSON. When the same seed is supplied with the same parameters, the output must be byte-for-byte identical across runs. The seed is passed directly to the underlying Faker random number generator (e.g., `Faker::Config.random = Random.new(seed)`). Features that do not consume randomness (e.g., pure lookup or slug formatting) still accept but may ignore the seed.

trigger_keywords: MAC address prefix
how to supply MAC prefix
mac_prefix format
partial MAC address
MAC vendor prefix
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The MAC address generator accepts an optional `mac_prefix` string in colon-separated hex format (e.g. `"fa:fa:fa"`). The generated MAC address must begin with the supplied prefix and append randomly generated octets to complete the six-octet address. If no prefix is supplied, all six octets are generated. The UUID output must be a version-4 UUID of exactly 36 characters including hyphens.

trigger_keywords: IBAN country code
how is IBAN formatted
iban_country input
IBAN length by country
bank identifier format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The IBAN generator accepts a country code string (case-insensitive, e.g. `"de"` or `"DE"`). The output `iban_country` line must be the uppercased two-letter code. The `iban_length` line must reflect the actual character count of the generated IBAN string for the given country (e.g. Germany produces 22-character IBANs). The account number is a zero-padded numeric string of exactly `account_digits` length.

trigger_keywords: file path separator
directory depth
root path input
how to configure file paths
file name format
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The file path generator accepts `depth` (integer number of directory levels), `root` (optional root string prepended to the directory, e.g. `"\\root\\"`), and `separator` (directory separator character, e.g. `"\\"`). The `file_name` output uses a forward-slash separator between directory and filename components regardless of the separator input. The `directory` output uses the supplied separator and prepends the root if provided.

trigger_keywords: phone number country code prefix
phone format
how is phone number assembled
cell phone vs regular phone
country dialing code
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The phone feature must output three lines: `country_code` (e.g. `+1`), `phone_with_country_code` (country code concatenated with a generated phone number, joined by a hyphen and space), and `cell_with_country_code` (country code and cell number joined by a space). The specific separators between country code and number differ: phone uses `{code}-{number}` and cell uses `{code} {number}`.

trigger_keywords: JSON shape output
nested JSON generation
how deep does JSON go
top_level_pairs meaning
flatten length calculation
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The JSON shape generator accepts `count` (number of top-level key-value pairs) and `depth` (nesting level, where depth=1 means each value is itself an object with `count` sub-pairs). The output `json` line contains the serialized JSON with no extra whitespace. `top_level_pairs` equals `count`. `top_level_flatten_length` equals twice the number of top-level keys (counting both key and value from the first nested level flattened), i.e. `count * 2` for depth=1.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: we had a bug with this in the old pipeline
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 6 (feature6_slug_and_url.json): slug normalization must strip all punctuation including dots and commas before joining with the glue string, not merely replace spaces

context_pointers.vague_reference: multiple sizing modes — exact counts, ranges, and pick-lists
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 10 (feature10_lorem_counts.json): the `characters`, `words`, `sentences`, and `paragraphs` fields each independently accept an integer, a `{min, max}` range hash, or an array of choices

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented in the ticket. Could you check the test case JSON files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the relevant feature, or look at the PRD in `start.md`? Those should have the exact expected input/output format you need.

feature10_lorem_counts.json.description: Generate placeholder text collections whose lengths follow exact, range, or choice-list count inputs.

cases.expected_output:
characters=5jd4c
characters_length=5
words=et|in|eligendi
words_count=3
sentences_count=2
paragraphs_count=1

feature11_color_values.json.description: Generate color values as names, hex strings, RGB arrays, HSL arrays, and HSLA arrays.

feature12_commerce_department.json.description: Generate commerce department labels with caller-controlled category counts and separators.

feature13_commerce_price.json.description: Generate a commerce price within default or supplied ranges and optionally render it as a string.

feature14_address_values.json.description: Generate postal address components and perform country code/name lookups.

longitude=2.059025084542867

feature15_bank_identifiers.json.description: Generate banking identifiers with caller-controlled account length and country-specific IBAN format.

feature16_company_identifiers.json.description: Generate company registration and tax identifiers with embedded checksum or format constraints.

feature17_file_paths.json.description: Generate file extensions, MIME types, complete file names, and directory paths with optional root and separator inputs.

feature18_phone_numbers.json.description: Generate phone country codes and international phone strings that begin with a country prefix.

feature19_json_shape.json.description: Generate shallow and nested JSON documents with a requested key/value generator and element count.

feature1_person_name.json.description: Generate a complete human name from the default person-name data set using a deterministic random seed.

feature20_error_normalization.json.description: Report language-neutral error categories when caller inputs make generation impossible or invalid.

feature2_person_initials.json.description: Generate uppercase initials where the caller controls the number of letters.

feature3_username.json.description: Create a lowercase internet username from caller-provided name text or length constraints.

feature4_password.json.description: Generate a password within requested length bounds with optional uppercase letters and symbols.

feature5_network_addresses.json.description: Generate internet addressing values including IPv4, CIDR, MAC, UUID, and user-agent strings.

feature6_slug_and_url.json.description: Normalize text into a URL slug and assemble a URL from scheme, domain, and path inputs.

feature7_fixed_length_number.json.description: Generate numeric strings with requested digit counts, including the zero-digit nil case.

feature8_numeric_ranges.json.description: Generate numbers constrained to inclusive, sign-forced, and reordered numeric bounds.

feature9_date_between_except.json.description: Generate a date inside a range while excluding a specified date.
